speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP] [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: We are continuing again with Erika Jacoby. Today is July 11, 1994. My name is Barbara Gordon. Erika, go on, tell us, continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here's the cup that I stole from this mansion that I destroyed before. What's interesting is that when arrived to America, and I met a cousin of mine, and I told him that I robbed and I stole, he was horrified that I did that. It-- I think it, it was, to him, incomprehensible that we would do such things, you know, break into houses and steal things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he come through this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was an American boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I think that gave me kind of a, a signal that I better don't tell him anything anymore. I, I wonder how much of this message got through to most of us, you know, that we might have done things that were not acceptable in normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: But these were not normal times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Miskolc, we, we were helped by the Joint Distribution Committee. And my mother decided to open a restaurant again. And with-- we got some of our furniture. I don't know from where. Some people found some of our furniture. And we brought it back, and we started to have a business. It lasted only a few months, but it, it is amazing that my mother was able to do that-- that she started her business. And my maid who brought me up came back just to welcome us. And she brought me a pillow cover, which I then made into a blouse. And that was the first clothing that I had after I returned. I, I don't remember how long I wore still the camp clothing when I came back, that funny pants that my friend made for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never took clothing when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you destroyed the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never took any clothing or jewelry or-- I mean, if I had taken some of those paintings, I, I would be able to survive for years after that. Never occurred to me. And other people did. Other people did, but it never occurred to me because it didn't mean to me anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I started to go to a group, which was a Zionist youth group called Bnei Akiva. And most of the kids who belonged there lost their parents, and this was kind of a first groping toward trying to overcome our losses. And they established also a summer camp, and I went to the summer camp, which was wonderful. And I remember so clearly that one night, we sat down the campfire and the question was asked, why did we survive? Just like what you said. How come we survived? And we were all 16, 17, 18 year olds, and we asked that question to which we could never find an answer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, I got up to Budapest. I left my mother in Miskolc and got up to Budapest because the Bnei Akiva had seminars there and programs, and I want to be more involved. And in Budapest, I learned also a trade of making handbags from leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was communist Hungary, occupied by the USSR. And I had to work in a place which was communist. And I remember people warning me that I was too free-speaking, that I should watch my language because I could be taken any time. I, I did not realize the, the, the problems with the Russians as much as-- in, in, in looking back I realize now. I know that the Russians came to our restaurant, and they used to drink a whole glass of vodka at one time and act kind of wild. But I-- and i remember being warned, be careful with them, you know? But I don't remember anything particularly harassing by them.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, I became active in this organization and went to another summer camp near the Lake Balaton, where I met my future husband. He was the camp director, and he was one of the leaders of the organization. He was the organizational leader. So we started going together, and eventually, we, we got engaged. The date of the engagement is very important. It was November 29, 1947. On that very day, he got his PhD from the University of Budapest. And the state of Israel was voted upon at the United Nations. So it was a three cel-- three-way celebration. The engagement party was at my aunt's house, and it was just for the family. And what was-- it was interesting to try to understand the logic of my mother. She did not really want me to get married, particularly. But she didn't have the heart to discourage me from this relationship. And my hus-- my, my fiance was a Zionist, a Zionist leader, and he wanted to go to Israel. And we made a deal that I go with my mother to Cuba because that's where my family sent visas to go to, and in two years, I will join him in Israel. That was our engagement pledge. And my aunts-- my uncles-- I had three uncles who had left Hungary before the war who were living in Mexico. They sent us papers. They wanted us to go to Mexico. But they wouldn't let us in there because they didn't let Jews in at that time. So they got us papers to go to Cuba. And in Cuba, 10 of us who left Hungary, five of us went to Cuba, and five of us went to Mexico. They were able to arrange the papers for my aunt and her three little children and a cousin of mine, a little four-year-old who became orphaned. Her father was killed during the war and her mother died just a few months before. So she was an orphan, and she joined my aunt. And they were able to get them in by adopt-- being adopted by my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Cuba-- my aunt, aunt and uncle, who had no children, my mother, and my brother. And we lived in Havana. And in Havana, this was-- it was like if you landed on the moon, as strange to us. It was a tropical country with colored people-- we had never seen colored people-- foreign language. I did study Spanish before we went there, so my Spanish was good enough so I got around.

speaker: interviewee
text: And but we were not allowed to work. We were not allowed to make a living. And the climate was very bad, very hot and humid. And we were living in one room-- my mother and my brother and I. My mother and I shared a bed, and my brother, the other bed. And we were renting a room from another refugee who had a bigger apartment. I, I, I worked clandestinely. I worked as a-- as a teacher. I taught Hebrew. I, I also taught Yiddish because that was demanded of me. In order to teach these children, I had to teach them in Yiddish, which I didn't know. So every night, I learned Yiddish, a lesson for the following day from a boy who I taught English, who-- which lesson I learned the day before from somebody else. So you could see how I tried to manipulate my life so I could somehow survive. Eventually, I was hired as a secretary of some sort-- also illegally-- and I learned to type. And then at the end, I was a peddler. I-- me and my brother went peddling jewelry, and we traveled across the country on a bus, and traveled with a little bag and tried to sell jewelry. My brother went to work on a farm and some other things. And my mother, she used to get from the farm eggs. And she carried this big basket of eggs from house to house. And she used to sell eggs. I corresponded with my fiance, and he asked me to go to Israel. And I could not leave my mother. I could not leave her in Mexico, and I could not part from her because I felt that I was still very much responsible. Not only that, I remember instructing my brother how she had--- how he had to behave nicer with my mother because we have to now be her happiness. And, and that became like the important thing in my life, an unattainable goal to make my mother happy and to make up for all the losses that she had suffered. Thinking back, I, I often remember that we survived probably in great part because we were together and because my mother didn't want me to become an orphan. And I didn't want her to lose another child. So that was kind of an unspoken agreement between us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, in, in-- I wanted to meet my fiance. I decided if I can't go to Israel, I will meet him wherever he is. And he came to the United States as a rabbinical student. He was brought out by the seminary. And I wanted to get into the United States hoping that if I'm in the United States, I can always have my mother come in, or I can eventually go to Mexico, where she's going to go. And I could not get a visa to get to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard about a pilot who was in the Air Force-- a United States Air Force who smuggled people in. And my mother got together $1,000. I don't know how-- how-- where she got together $1,000. And I and with two other boys, we hired this pilot who met us in a sugar field in the middle of the night. He landed his small airplane in the eastern part of Cuba where we travelled by bus. And I went there without anything, just with whatever I was wearing. And we got on this little airplane the second time when he tried. He tried the first time, and he had to take off because there was some danger. The second time, we got on this airplane, and we got-- we got up. And I was very sick on the airplane. And I saw this oil dripping on the wings, and I was scared. And I saw this Air Force man with this big gun, and I wasn't sure I will ever get to land. But we did land somewhere in Florida. And his wife picked us up right at the airplane and took us to her house. We were locked into the closet until midnight, where he-- when he took us to the airport and bought-- we had to give him money to buy us tickets to New York. I went with-- as a wife of one of the boys. I don't remember what name I had. And we travelled to New York on Eastern Airlines. It was a very scary night because before we went to the airport, they took us to a restaurant to eat, and this Air Force pilot got drunk and started to sing. And, and I was sitting there, very tense and terrified. Anyway, we arrived to New York early in the morning, and we took a taxi. This boy and me took a taxi, and the boy dropped me off at my cousin's house. And he took the taxi to his house. My cousins had no idea how I got there. I, I knocked on the door and I told them, please don't ask me how I got here. Just let me have a bed and so I could sleep. And they didn't ask me. And the next day, I told them that I want to meet my fiance. And they called him up, and they told him to come over to discuss something. They didn't tell him that I was there. And I decided to go out on the street and wait for him at the subway station. And as he took off from the subway, I just tapped him from the back. And I said, hi. Well, it was very dramatic. He, he was a student, and he was also teaching at that time in the Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed with my aunt for a couple of weeks. And then they didn't feel comfortable, me being there illegally, and we-- I decided to move out. And I found a room to rent from another family, somewhere uptown Manhattan near the-- on Riverside drive, across from the Palisades in New Jersey. It was a beautiful place. And I rented a little maid's room. It was very narrow, just a bed, a desk, and, and a closet in it. And there was a little half bath.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day, I asked my, my fiance, let's go out and, and look for some books. Because he had some money because he earned. And we went out to the Lower East Side, and we bought about 20 books. And I put them on top of my desk because I had to have books. I bought Virgil, and American literature, and English literature. None, none of-- none of this I could read, but I wanted them. And then a few weeks later, I got a job. And I decided to become Eva Heller. And I got a social security. And I worked illegally. And one day, the cousin called me and said, in the Jewish paper there is an article that they caught the pilot. And the pilot is giving away the names that he had brought in, and be careful. And one day, I went out with my fiance to the place where he taught on Shabbat and stayed with him over the weekend. And before I came home, I always telephoned first. And the people who live there, said in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. That means don't come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened is that the young man I came with somehow knew-- he was caught and knew where my aunt wa-- where my cousin was. And they went to my cousin's house. And she told them, my cousin, that she doesn't know where I was. Then they somehow found my apartment. I don't know how. And they came to the room. And they searched my room, and they found all these books. And they made a list of my books.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very important to them because they thought I was a spy. They thought I was a spy and that was the Hung-- the communist craziness, you know, the fear of the communists that I was-- because many of the communists were smuggled in. And many Chinese were smuggled in such a way. And so that-- the, the people told me not to come home. They later told me the next day when I could talk to them a little that they even-- I had by then a coat-- that they even cut the dress, the bottom of the dress, to see if I had anything hidden in the seams. Unfortunately, they found an-- my address book. And so now they had an address. And they had Uzi's, or Emil's address in it in the seminary. So the FBI went to the seminary and got him. And they said, now we know that you were in touch with your fiance. You get in the car with us now, and let's go through all these addresses and see if we can find her because I am a very much wanted person.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went to all of the places that I could hide. They went to all of these places to look for me. And I could not stay in any place more than one night. I was hiding every night someplace else. We used to meet, Emil and I, on the, on the subway. We used to kind of try to get away to communicate with each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I had gotten ill. It was winter already, and I got ill. And I had-- I used to walk on the street from morning until night because I had no place to be. I could not go back to work anymore once they decided that I was-- I was esca-- you know, illegally there. And when I-- I got pneumonia. Eventually, I got pneumonia, and I had to find a place to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to one of the cousins, the Horowitz-Margareten cousins who I knew. And it was some kind of a, I don't know, dinner, and I was-- I went there. And he said to me, you know what? We are your cousins. Let us help you. I think you have to give yourself up. You can't go on hiding like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, meanwhile, I-- this is so interesting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Meanwhile, I left New York, and I went to New Jersey to stay with an old boyfriend of mine who housed me with his fian-- with his girlfriends. And I stayed there for a few days. And that's how-- and eventually, I couldn't stay there longer. I had to come back. But anyway, when I went to the cousin's house, he told me that he has-- we have another cousin who is an attorney who will talk to the FBI and see if he can make a deal. And so indeed, he did. And I had another cousin who was a doctor who then gave me some medication. So I felt, finally, that I had some people who'd take care of me. And they-- one day, I and two lawyers, two attorneys-- just like you see it on television-- went up-- I don't know the legal--

speaker: interviewer
text: Terminology.

speaker: interviewee
text: --terminology-- went up, and I was arrested. And I was guided by a female officer and couldn't go to the bathroom without her. And anyway, they made a deal. They put down some kind of a bond, a bail of I don't know how many thousands of dollars, and they released me at the end of the day. And I was to be released and to report once a month. And with even-- the plan to be eventuality deported-- deported, either back to Cuba or to Germany, or to Japan. These were the countries that lost the war. They had to take in refugees. And now started the odyssey of two years of trying to avoid deportation, of trying to find help, legal help, money, eventually a congressman who-- meanwhile, I got married-- we got married in 19-- in September 1950. And cousins helped me to make a wedding. And I got pregnant, and I had a child who died four days later. And still, I was illegal. And every month I was asked to report to Ellis Island. And either I couldn't go because I was pregnant, or I had a child, or I was ill, or some other ways I was able to get out of that. Eventually, Emil got a visa as a professor. A visa that only-- that expired on December 25, 1952. He went to pick up the visa in November, and then, as a wife, I was able to get the visa. But I couldn't get that either because in order to go to Canada to pick up the visa, I had to have a return visa here. I couldn't get a return visa because I was illegal here. That's when the congressman finally got me through, that I was able to get a return via, a provisional return visa-- if I got a visa, a, a normal visa. I left New York on December 23. I slept over in Montreal. I went to the embassy on December 24 to pick up my visa, which I did. I had a visa. I went to the airport in the evening. All flights were cancelled to the United-- to New York because of a snowstorm. I could not get on the airplane. I had to enter the country before the 25th, otherwise, the visa expired. I got to the-- a friend of ours took me to the railroad station. I got on a train, and I was able to cross the border to the United States at 11:45 before the visa expired. And that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you're here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I'm here, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You have three wonderful sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I, I lived in Brooklyn, New York until 1953. I worked as a bookkeeper. I got a job lying-- by lying that I had two years of experience when I didn't even know anything about bookkeeping or, or even English that well. I, I got pregnant again after the loss of that baby after I got my visa. And we came to Los-- to Los Angeles because Emil got a job at Adat Ari El, which was called VJCC at that time. And this is where our life began here. I-- we had three children, three wonderful children, who are now all living somewhere else. And I have seven grandchildren. And I only see them once in awhile. But somehow, I gave them the message that it was OK for them to leave me, so they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your mother is still here.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is still here, and I don't think we will ever leave each other. And I eventually-- I became a Hebrew teacher, and I taught in-- I taught in, in Hebrew school as well as at camp. And it was at camp where I started to talk about my experiences way before anybody else talked about it because I somehow wanted to get closer to the children. And because of that experience, that I needed to connect to people, I think I worked through a lot of this grief that I had. Eventually, I went to college. I didn't go back to college, I went to college. I lied about high school, which I never finished. And eventually, I went to USC, and I got my degree in clinical social work. And I've been working as a clinical social worker for the past 20 years, 25 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can probably turn it around a little bit. That's good. Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my great grandfather, Yitzhak Horowitz, in 1903 at his-- on his deathbed. He was 53 years old. And that's why he allowed to have a picture taken of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my great grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my grandparents from my mother's side, Joseph Salamonovits and Julia Salamonovits.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that you should-- this is the grandfather you carried?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the grandfather that I carried down. This is my father's father. His name was Engel, Farkas. And he was blind when he was taken, and, of course, killed immediately. These are my parents. My father, his name was Jeno Engel, Jacov [INAUDIBLE], and my mother, Malvina, Miriam, Engel. It was taken in 1936 at my aunt's wedding. These are the Engel children, my oldest broth-- older brother, Yitzhak or Zoltan, my younger brother Tibor, or Moshe, and I, the only girl. I think I was four years old, and my brother fi-- four-- my brother-- little brother three, and my older brother, six. This is my graduation pictures-- picture from the Israelite middle school in Miskolc, and this is me. It was in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a great shot too. That's a graduation picture-- or no, engagement.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is our engagement picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture on the Queen Mary as we left London for the United States. This is my mother. This is me. And my brother over there. This was in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Point one more time.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That's me. This is my mother. This is my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding-- our wedding-- in 1950, September 24, and my uncle and aunt. This is our wedding picture from September 24, 1950 with my uncle and aunt and Emil's aunt and cousin. These were the closest relatives who were there.
